text: Single-atom catalysts with a well-defined active metal site have triggered huge interest in exploring the catalytic active site and reaction mechanism of various chemical transformations. While the understanding of the electronic and structural dynamic behaviors of single-atom catalytic sites under operational conditions remains limited, which is mainly attributed to the fact that most operando probing techniques cannot directly monitor the sites and have been extensively applied to multi-atom-based single catalytic materials. Here, we for the first time combined operando techniques to explore the structural dynamic behavior and the electronic states of single-atom catalytic sites during the entire electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) process in a single iron atom based model catalyst. Our operando 57Fe Mössbauer and X-ray absorption spectroscopy experiments confirm that the well-defined iron sites in resting state are in high-spin (HS) Fe(III)N4 species, which are reduced to HS Fe(II)N4 at decreasing potentials or under CO2 or Ar saturated electrolyte solution, with different adsorbates and stabilities. Meanwhile, by utilizing operando Raman spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry techniques, the phthalocyanine (Pc) ligand coordinated to the iron cation is identified to undergo a redox process from Fe(II)Pc to Fe(II)Pc-. As a result, the HS Fe(II)Pc- species is identified as the catalytic intermediate for CO2RR. Additional theoretical calculations confirm that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand leads to a different d-band center for the in situ generated HS Fe(II)Pc- species, which has an optimal binding energy for CO2 toward the enhancement of CO2RR performance. Therefore, this research provides not only experimental and theoretical evidence for the electronic structural and dynamic behaviors of single catalytic sites on single-atom-Fe materials, but also guides the design of efficient CO2RR catalysts.
